sources.name: TASVideos Game Resources — NES / Super Mario Bros
sources.notes: Primary community resource. Documents 21-frame rule, gravity types, walljump thresholds.

sources.name: Data Crystal — Super Mario Bros. RAM Map
sources.notes: Authoritative ROM address documentation. Source for all $00xx addresses in this file.

sources.name: A Comprehensive Super Mario Bros. Disassembly (doppelganger)
sources.notes: Full annotated 6502 assembly disassembly. Primary verification source for all constants.

sources.name: SDA Knowledge Base — Super Mario Bros.
sources.notes: Speedrunning physics documentation. Walk max 1.5 px/frame, run max 2.5 px/frame confirmed here.

notes: SMB1 uses a 16-sub-pixel system for position: 16 sub-pixels = 1 pixel.
Gravity and acceleration use a 256-sub-pixel internal accumulator.
The 'feel' of SMB1 physics comes from the 4-state gravity system — which gravity applies depends on how fast Mario was moving when he jumped.
NES runs at 60.0988 fps NTSC (treated as 60fps). PAL at 50fps.
Standard NES screen: 256x240 pixels. ~8px overscan top and bottom = 224 visible lines in practice.

units.speed: sub-pixels per frame (16 sub-px = 1 pixel for position)
units.gravity: units of 1/256 pixel per frame squared (gravity accumulator uses 256-sub-px precision)

subpixel_system.description: SMB1 uses two different precision levels: 16 sub-pixels for position/speed display, 256 sub-pixels for gravity accumulation. The fractional position byte accumulates gravity in 1/256 increments.

display.notes: Standard NES. 8x8 CHR tiles, but gameplay grid uses 16x16 blocks.

walk_max_speed.source: SDA Knowledge Base + disassembly. Walk max = 1.5 px/frame confirmed by multiple speedrun sources.
walk_max_speed.notes: B button NOT held. Mario walks at up to 24/16 = 1.5 px/frame.

run_max_speed.source: Data Crystal RAM map: $0700 'Player speed in either direction (0 - 0x28)'. 0x28 = 40 sub-px/frame = 2.5 px/frame.
run_max_speed.notes: B button held. 0x28 = 40 sub-pixels = 2.5 px/frame. This is confirmed by both RAM map and community documentation.

acceleration.description: SMB1 uses three acceleration values depending on speed relative to thresholds.

values.condition: Speed below walk threshold, same direction
Speed above walk threshold, same direction

values.notes: Skid deceleration is faster than acceleration — Mario stops quickly when reversing direction.

acceleration.notes: Acceleration magnitude also doubles when Mario is NOT facing his direction of movement.

max_left_speed.source: Data Crystal RAM map: $0450 '$E4 max walk left, $D8 max run left'

initial_y_velocity.description: Y velocity stored as signed byte. $FB = 251 unsigned = -5 signed = 5 px/frame upward.
initial_y_velocity.source: Data Crystal RAM map: '$009F - Player vertical velocity (whole pixels, signed byte). Upward: 0xFB = normal jump'
initial_y_velocity.notes: This is the whole-pixel Y speed. Fractional Y speed also exists at $0433.

max_fall_speed.description: Terminal velocity: 5 px/frame downward. Symmetric with jump initial velocity.
max_fall_speed.source: Data Crystal RAM map: '$009F ... Downward: 0x05 = fastest fall'

gravity_system.description: SMB1 has 4 distinct gravity states. The state is determined at jump initiation based on Mario's X speed. Mario KEEPS that gravity state until the next jump.
gravity_system.source: TASVideos: 'There are 4 types of gravity in SMB. Mario's gravity is affected by his most recent jump. He keeps that gravity state until he jumps again.'

standing_or_very_slow.source: Data Crystal: $0709='$30 standing jump gravity', $070A='$A8 standing fall gravity'

walking.source: Data Crystal: $0709='$2D walking jump gravity', $070A='$90 walking fall gravity'

running.source: Data Crystal: $0709='$38 running jump gravity', $070A='$D0 running fall gravity'

swimming_floating.description: Low gravity used for swimming states and initial spawn/pipe-exit float.
swimming_floating.notes: Much lower gravity than any land jump state. Responsible for the 'floaty' feel when entering levels via pipes.

gravity_system.important_note: The 'running' jump has HIGHER gravity than the 'walking' jump. This means running jumps are faster-arcing but the horizontal distance from speed compensates. The key insight: run-jumping FEELS higher because horizontal speed carries Mario farther, but the arc is more abrupt.

variable_jump.mechanic: Holding A while Y velocity is negative (ascending) keeps the lower jump gravity active. Releasing A switches to the higher falling gravity, cutting the jump short.
variable_jump.notes: Short hops are possible by tapping A. Full jumps require holding A until apex.

level_transitions.source: TASVideos: '21 frame rule: level transitions round up to next 21-frame boundary'
level_transitions.notes: This is one of the most famous optimization constraints in speedrunning. Entering a flagpole or pipe rounds the timer up to the nearest 21-frame multiple. Speedrunners optimize to save full 21-frame cycles.

small_mario.notes: Small Mario: 1 tile tall, half tile wide.

hitboxes.notes: Hitboxes are 8px wide — narrower than the 16px sprite. This gives 4px of forgiveness on each side, a key reason the game feels fair.

underwater_physics.gravity_reduction: Uses the swimming/float gravity state (much lower than land states)

swim_speed.notes: Walking underwater speed is 1 px/frame — significantly slower than surface walk max of 1.5 px/frame.

underwater_physics.notes: Underwater levels have dramatically reduced gravity and max fall speed. Mario swims by repeatedly pressing A (each press applies upward impulse).

invincibility_frames.notes: Star invincibility lasts approximately 10.7 seconds (640 frames at 60fps). Standard hit from enemies: Mario loses size (big→small) or dies (small). No temporary invincibility from a standard hit.
invincibility_frames.hit_invincibility: No invincibility from standard enemy hits — Mario immediately changes state.

design_notes_for_giants_drink: The 4-gravity system is the secret ingredient of SMB1's physics feel. Many clones miss this: they implement one gravity value and wonder why it feels wrong. The gravity at jump time must be captured and held.
Running jumps go FARTHER but not necessarily higher than walking jumps (similar initial Y velocity, but the arc lasts longer due to horizontal carry). This is the correct mental model.
The 8px hitbox width is what makes SMB1 feel fair — enemies can clip corners of sprites without registering a hit. Preserve this forgiveness.
The 21-frame rule is a level-transition artifact, not a physics constant. Don't implement it — it's an emergent property of the NES timing loop.
Mario's acceleration model has three values (slow, medium, skid) — this is what creates the 'weight' feel. Too-simple acceleration produces a floaty, arcade-y Mario that doesn't feel right.
